message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

10 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] When time-compressed to the same fast rate, normal speech is MORE intelligible than clear (hyperarticulated) speech — meaning the intelligibility advantage of clear-speech corpora does not survive aggressive time-compression and can even invert. This directly bears on whether training on clear-speech corpora is worthwhile for a fast-listening pipeline.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 (primary)
Quote: "Even when controlling for duration, compressed normal speech is still more intelligible than compressed clear speech."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported by the primary source and corroborated independently. The source is arXiv 1901.07239, "Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates" by Valentini-Botinhao, Wester, Yamagishi, Toman, Pucher & Schabus (Centre for Speech Technology Research, Univ. of Edinburgh — established speech/TTS researchers; not marketing or a forum). WebFetch of the abstract and multiple web searches confirm the supporting quote verbatim: "compressed normal speech is more intelligible than compressed clear speech even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same across styles." This directly supports both the core claim (normal > clear under aggressive time-compression) and the duration-controlled framing. The "inversion" framing is also corroborated: a search result states "Unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed clear speech at moderate rates, but at the highest speaking rates clear speech was worse than fast speech" — i.e., the advantage holds at moderate rates and inverts only at the highest compression rates. IMPORTANT NUANCE (not a refutation): Krause & Braida (2002, JASA, PubMed 12430828) find that NATURALLY PRODUCED clear speech retains its advantage even at faster natural speaking rates ("clear speech has inherent acoustic properties that contribute to high intelligibility without altering rate"). This is a different mechanism (natural fast production) from signal-domain time-compression of pre-recorded slow clear speech, which is exactly the claim's scope, so it delineates rather than contradicts the claim. The finding is a stable psychoacoustic result, not a fast-moving benchmark, so the 2019 date is not a staleness concern. Two practical caveats on STRENGTH: (a) the inversion is specific to AGGRESSIVE/high compression rates, not all fast rates; (b) this is one study, so "can even invert" is appropriately hedged. The claim as worded ("does not survive aggressive time-compression and CAN even invert") matches the evidence's strength.

### [1] Natively-recorded fast speech is LESS intelligible than a normal-rate voice that is linearly time-compressed to the same rate, suggesting post-hoc time-scale modification of a normal voice can beat naturally-produced fast speech for intelligibility — relevant to the native-fast-generation vs. time-compression trade-off (angle 3).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 (primary)
Quote: "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and verbatim supported by the primary source. arXiv:1901.07239 (a preprint of work in Speech Communication on time-compressed speech) states in its abstract: "unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech." This is exactly the claim. The finding is corroborated by multiple independent peer-reviewed studies: (a) Janse et al., "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech" (Speech Communication, ScienceDirect S0167639303001092) found natural fast speech is less intelligible, partly due to reduced vowel space and uneven (non-linear) segment shortening; (b) the 2016 Springer perception study (10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x) found natural fast speech is perceived as faster and harder to process than linearly compressed speech; transfer of perceptual adaptation only went from compressed to natural, not vice versa. CRUCIALLY for the TTS/synthetic application domain, the study "Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style" (Speech Communication, ScienceDirect S0167639315000977) found that "generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates" for English voices — directly extending the claim to synthetic/TTS speech and supporting the time-compression-beats-native-fast conclusion. No contradicting credible source was found. QUALIFICATIONS (do not refute, but bound the claim): (1) The arXiv finding concerns NATURAL human speech, not neural-TTS native-fast generation; the claim correctly hedges ("relevant to," "suggesting") rather than over-asserting TTS evidence. (2) The synthetic study adds a talker-dependent nuance: a skilled German speaker who articulated cleanly at fast rate (few deletions/substitutions) yielded intelligible results when his fast data was compressed, meaning the advantage of "normal-then-compress" is not absolute but depends on how cleanly the fast version is articulated. Source quality (peer-reviewed Speech Communication / Springer), recency-stability (psychoacoustics, not a fast-moving benchmark), and convergent multi-study support all match the claim's strength. Sources: https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.07239 ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977 ; https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092 ; https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x

### [2] Greater dynamic formant movement (linear movement between vowel nucleus endpoints, VL) predicts higher vowel intelligibility, whereas non-linear formant movement within the nucleus does not — meaning preserving formant transitions/movement is what carries vowel identity, not just static targets.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4048446/ (primary)
Quote: "Greater linear formant movement between the endpoints of the vowel nucleus (VL) was associated with higher vowel intelligibility, while non-linear movement within the nucleus (TLadj) was unrelated to vowel intelligibility"
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source PMC4048446 (Neel et al., "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners," peer-reviewed, PubMed 24907820, 2014) directly and verbatim supports the claim. WebFetch confirmed the GLMM results: VL (linear formant movement between 20%-80% nucleus endpoints in F1xF2 space) beta=0.576, p<0.0001 (highly significant positive effect on intelligibility); TLadj (non-linear deviation of the trajectory from the straight-line vector VL) beta=-0.032, n.s. (unrelated to intelligibility). Study sample: 41 talkers, 7 young normal-hearing + 40 elderly hearing-impaired listeners, 10 English vowels in /bVd/ context, clear vs conversational speech. The finding held for BOTH listener groups (VL effect slightly stronger for young NH, marginal group interaction p=0.0684 -- a nuance, not a contradiction). The interpretive extension ('preserving formant transitions/movement carries vowel identity, not just static targets') is independently corroborated by the broader Vowel Inherent Spectral Change (VISC) literature (Springer 'Vowel Inherent Spectral Change' 2013; JASA studies on VISC): dynamic-change models classify vowel categories far more accurately than steady-state models, and flat-formant (static) vowels show large identification-accuracy drops. No contradicting source found in two targeted searches. Source quality (peer-reviewed JASA-tier primary study with proper mixed-effects analysis) matches the claim's strength; the topic is foundational acoustic phonetics, not a fast-moving field, so 2014 publication date does not make it outdated.</parameter>
</invoke>


### [3] Longer vowel duration improves intelligibility for both listener groups but especially for elderly hearing-impaired listeners, by giving more processing time; clear speech lengthened vowels by a factor of ~1.5 (290 ms vs 204 ms).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4048446/ (primary)
Quote: "Longer vowel duration helped both groups but especially EHI listeners, perhaps by allowing more processing time for vowel identifications."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against the primary source (Ferguson & Quené, "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners," J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 135(6):3570-3584, 2014 — peer-reviewed, PMC4048446). Every element of the claim matches the paper verbatim: (1) Listener groups = young normal-hearing (YNH, 19-30) vs elderly hearing-impaired (EHI, 65-87), matching "both groups... especially elderly hearing-impaired." (2) Mechanism: paper states "Longer vowel duration helped both groups but especially EHI listeners, perhaps by allowing more processing time for vowel identifications" — exact match including "more processing time." (3) Numbers: paper reports "average vowel durations of 290 ms in clear speech and 204 ms in conversational speech" and "vowel duration increased in clear speech by a factor of 1.5" — exact match to "~1.5 (290 ms vs 204 ms)." Source quality (JASA, top-tier acoustics journal) exceeds the bar for this claim. Not outdated: vowel lengthening in clear speech is a foundational, decades-replicated finding in speech acoustics (cf. Picheny et al. 1986), not a fast-moving field. Not marketing/cherry-picked — empirical study. One minor unstated nuance: the paper notes the per-vowel duration effect on identification odds was significant "only for tense vowels," which qualifies but does not contradict the directional claim (and the claim's own "perhaps"/"by giving more processing time" mirrors the paper's hedged phrasing).

### [4] Clear speech expands the vowel space: front-vowel F2 increases and back-vowel F2 decreases, with F2 range expanding ~14% and F1 range ~9% — quantifying the vowel-space-expansion / hyperarticulation effect that aids intelligibility.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4048446/ (primary)
Quote: "The F2 range expanded by an average of 14% (0.38 Bark increase), with "F1 range expansion of approximately 9% (0.23 Bark).""
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (Ferguson & Quené, PMC4048446, "Acoustic correlates of vowel intelligibility in clear and conversational speech for young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners") verbatim confirms every number in the claim. F2: "The clear/conversational ratio for F2 range was 1.14 on average [...] the 14% expansion corresponds with an average F2 range increase of 0.38 Bark]". F1: "ratio for F1 range was 1.09 on average [...] the 9% expansion corresponds with an average increase of the F1 range by 0.23 Bark]". Both significant (F2: t(40)=5.6, p<0.0001; F1: t(40)=4.3, p=0.0001). The directional pattern is also confirmed: "the front vowels /i/, /ɪ/, /e/, /ε/, /æ/ showed significantly higher F2 values in clear speech, while the back vowels /o/, /ʊ/, /u/ had significantly lower F2 values." Source quality is high (peer-reviewed JASA-class study, NIH PMC). Only nuance: the "aids intelligibility" framing is association, not universal causation — this paper found expansion improved vowel intelligibility for young normal-hearing listeners but NOT for elderly hearing-impaired listeners. That qualification does not refute the claim, which targets the general-student/podcast (normal-hearing) use case and is correctly characterized as quantifying the hyperarticulation effect.

### [5] Clear speech (deliberate hyperarticulation) produces a measurable intelligibility advantage over conversational speech for consonants/fricatives, with intelligibility benefits in prior literature ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points; in this study clear speech significantly lowered SNR identification thresholds for both normal-hearing and simulated-hearing-impaired listeners.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2007/maniwa07_interspeech.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Clear speech elicited in laboratory settings has been shown to benefit a variety of listener populations under different adverse conditions (e.g. noise, reverberation) [e.g. 3, 4, 5], resulting in intelligibility advantages relative to “conversational” speech ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against primary source. Extracted the exact text from the Maniwa, Jongman & Wade (2007) Interspeech PDF (isca-archive.org). The verbatim sentence reads: "Clear speech elicited in laboratory settings has been shown to benefit a variety of listener populations under different adverse conditions (e.g. noise, reverberation) [e.g. 3, 4, 5], resulting in intelligibility advantages relative to 'conversational' speech ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points." This matches the claim's '7 to 38 percentage points' figure exactly. The SNR-threshold finding is independently confirmed by the peer-reviewed JASA publication of the same study (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade 2008, J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 123(2):1114-1125, PubMed 18247912): "Two experiments measured babble signal-to-noise ratio thresholds for fricative minimal pair distinctions for 14 normal-hearing listeners and 14 listeners with simulated sloping, recruiting impairment. Results indicated that clear speech helped both groups overall." The study is explicitly about fricative consonants. MINOR QUALIFICATION (not a refutation): the abstract notes "for impaired listeners, reliable clear speech intelligibility advantages were not found for non-sibilant pairs" — i.e., the benefit for impaired listeners was overall but not universal across all fricative pairs. The claim does not assert universality, so this is a nuance rather than a contradiction. Source quality (ISCA peer-reviewed conference + JASA journal) matches claim strength; age (2007/2008) is fine for foundational acoustic-phonetics science. Note: also independently observed that the SAME paper immediately adds a robustness caveat ("some results call into question the robustness of the 'clear speech' effect... negative clear-speech benefits in elderly hearing-impaired listeners"), but this does not undermine the specific claim under review.

### [6] For normal-hearing listeners, the dominant acoustic modification driving the clear-speech intelligibility gain in fricatives is a shift of spectral energy toward higher frequency regions, evidenced by significant positive correlations between intelligibility advantage and DFT/LPC spectral-peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2007/maniwa07_interspeech.pdf (primary)
Quote: "Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1)... These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement."
Verifier evidence (high): Verified the supporting quote verbatim by extracting raw text directly from the primary PDF (cached at the local tool-results path), not relying on WebFetch summary. Exact text in the paper: "Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1), whereas significant negative correlations were found for M3. These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement." The abstract scopes this precisely to the right population: "a shift of energy concentration toward higher frequency regions and greater source strength contributed to the clear speech effect for normal-hearing listeners, while listeners with simulated loss seemed to benefit mostly from cues involving lower frequency regions." The claim correctly limits itself to NORMAL-HEARING listeners and does NOT overgeneralize (the paper found the opposite direction for simulated hearing-impaired listeners), so it avoids the obvious refutation. Source is peer-reviewed (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade, Interspeech 2007), authored by established speech-perception researchers, and independently corroborated by the companion peer-reviewed paper Maniwa, Jongman & Wade (2008), J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 123(2):1114 ("Perception of clear fricatives by normal-hearing and simulated hearing-impaired listeners"). No contradicting source found in web search. The finding is a settled phonetics result (not a fast-moving field), so 2007 dating is not a weakness. Minor caveat (non-refuting): the word "dominant" paraphrases the paper's "most closely related"/"important predictors," and the paper also credits "greater source strength" alongside the frequency shift; the claim's correlational framing ("evidenced by significant positive correlations") matches the paper's correlational (non-causal) methodology.

### [7] The temporal modulations most important for speech intelligibility lie in the 1-7 Hz range, with spectral modulations below ~1 cycle/kHz being most important; the speech modulation transfer function is bandpass in the temporal domain and low-pass in the spectral domain.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pcbi.1000302 (primary)
Quote: "temporal modulations from 1 to 7 Hz and spectral modulations <1 cycles/kHz were the most important ... the MTF was bandpass in temporal modulations and low-pass in spectral modulations"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim supported by the primary source. Elliott & Theunissen (2009), "The Modulation Transfer Function for Speech Intelligibility," PLoS Computational Biology 5(3):e1000302, PMID 19266016 (UC Berkeley, Helen Wills Neuroscience Institute) states in its abstract: "the MTF was bandpass in temporal modulations and low-pass in spectral modulations: temporal modulations from 1 to 7 Hz and spectral modulations <1 cycles/kHz were the most important." This matches the supporting quote exactly. PubMed and the PMC full-text (PMC2639724) independently confirm identical wording. The paper additionally notes comprehension is significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz are removed (a broader necessary band), which is consistent with — not contradictory to — the narrower "most important" core. Cross-checked against the wider literature: the consensus that intelligibility-critical envelope information sits in the low-frequency temporal band (roughly 1-16 Hz, peaking near the ~4 Hz syllabic rate; STI/Houtgast-Steeneken tradition, Drullman 1994, Greenberg) is fully compatible with the 1-7 Hz range. The only genuine debates in the literature concern the precise peak (4 Hz vs band) and the exact upper edge, neither of which refutes the bandpass-temporal/low-pass-spectral characterization as stated. Source quality is high: peer-reviewed, foundational, highly cited primary research using a novel joint spectrotemporal modulation-filtering technique — not marketing, forum, or cherry-picked benchmark. The 2009 date reflects settled psychoacoustics, not a fast-moving field, so it is not outdated. No credible contradicting source was found.

### [8] There exists a core spectro-temporal modulation region below ~8 Hz temporally and ~4 cycles/kHz spectrally that is essential for speech comprehension; comprehension is significantly impaired when modulations below these cutoffs are removed.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pcbi.1000302 (primary)
Quote: "there exists a region, or 'core', of modulations below ∼4 cycles/kHz and ∼8 Hz that are essential for comprehension"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is verbatim-supported by the primary source. Elliott & Theunissen (2009), "The Modulation Transfer Function for Speech Intelligibility," PLOS Computational Biology (DOI: 10.1371/journal.pcbi.1000302), states in its conclusion: "the results from the spectral and temporal domains suggested that there exists a region, or 'core', of modulations below ∼4 cycles/kHz and ∼8 Hz that are essential for comprehension." The abstract independently confirms the impairment direction and significance: "Comprehension was significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz were removed." Checklist results: (1) Quote-support — exact match; the claim restates the paper's own integrated "core" cutoffs (~8 Hz, ~4 cycles/kHz) and the significant-impairment finding, no overreach. The ~8 Hz figure is the paper's own synthesized core boundary (the 12 Hz is the initial low-pass impairment onset; the paper itself collapses these to ~8 Hz). (2) No contradicting evidence found; the result is mainstream and consistent with the broader MTF/STI literature (syllabic rate ~4-7 Hz). (3) Source quality high — primary, peer-reviewed, highly cited journal article, matching the claim's strength. (4) Not meaningfully outdated — this is foundational human-auditory psychophysics (filtering + transcription-in-noise listening tests), not a fast-moving engineering benchmark; the biology is unchanged. (5) Not marketing/forum/cherry-picked — rigorous academic psychophysics study. Verified via PMC mirror (PMC2639724) and PLOS original.

### [9] Inserting silent gaps ('repackaging') into time-compressed speech at theta-compatible rates restores intelligibility, with the optimal restoration ('rescue point') at ~5 packets/second for young adults (center of the 3-8 Hz theta range). This means rhythmic restructuring, not just raw rate, governs comprehensibility at speed.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6181647/ (primary)
Quote: "the rescue point for the young adults occurred at 5 packets per second, at the center of the typical theta frequency range for young adults (3 to 8 Hz), while the rescue point for the older adults occurred at 4 packets per second"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is accurately supported by its primary source, PMC6181647 ("The possible role of brain rhythms in perceiving fast speech: Evidence from adult aging," Journal of the Acoustical Society of America, Oct 2018 — peer-reviewed primary research). The supporting quote is verbatim accurate: the paper defines the "rescue point" as the earliest tested rate at which repackaging (dividing time-compressed speech into packets delivered with inserted silent gaps) yielded significantly better digit-recognition than continuous time-compression — 5 packets/s for young adults (center of the 3-8 Hz theta range) and 4 packets/s for older adults. The methodology (digit recognition accuracy across 6-21 syll/s, repackaged at 2-7 packets/s) measures intelligibility directly, supporting that this is a comprehensibility finding. The core conceptual claim — that rhythmic restructuring, not raw rate alone, governs intelligibility — is the established finding of the Ghitza/Greenberg line of work: repackaged speech at the same information rate is markedly more intelligible than continuously compressed speech (poor ~50% WER without gaps, restored with 20-120 ms gaps). No contradicting source was found. The one qualifying paper (Nature Scientific Reports 2023, s41598-023-29755-x) is complementary, not contradicting: it argues the benefit may ALSO involve temporal-fine-structure processing in addition to theta synchronization — qualifying the causal MECHANISM but not the behavioral rescue-point finding. The only soft caveat is that theta-oscillation CAUSATION is presented as the TEMPO hypothesis/correlational framework rather than proven causation, but the claim hedges appropriately ("theta-compatible rates") and its load-bearing facts (rescue point = 5/s for young adults; rhythm > raw rate) are directly and correctly sourced.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "Speech intelligibility depends on whether the input rhythm stays within the brain's theta-band frequency range (3-8 Hz); when input rate exceeds theta, intelligibility sharply decreases. This sets a neural ceiling on raw fast-speech comprehension that time-compression alone cannot overcome." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6181647/, vote 1-2)
- "Hyperarticulated (clear) speech was rated easier to understand than non-hyperarticulated speech across all listeners and all listening conditions (quiet through +8 dB SNR noise), with a very large effect (F(1,45)=205.002, p<0.05, partial eta-squared=0.820)." (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00426-021-01595-2, vote 0-1)
- "The hyperarticulation benefit to comprehension persisted across multiple adverse-noise levels (quiet, +16 dB, +12 dB, +8 dB SNR), demonstrating that clear-speech intelligibility gains survive signal degradation." (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00426-021-01595-2, vote 1-0)
- "Blind people who train in ultra-fast speech comprehension can understand spoken language at up to ~22 syllables per second, roughly 2.5-3x the maximum rate of normal-sighted listeners (~8 syl/s)." (https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74, vote 0-0)
- "Trained blind (vision-impaired) listeners can learn to comprehend accelerated synthetic speech at rates up to approximately 22 syllables per second, far exceeding the ~8 syllables/second upper limit of normal sighted listeners." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/, vote 0-0)
- "The ultra-fast speech-listening experiments with blind subjects used the Eloquence formant synthesizer (via the JAWS screen reader), the same class of formant synthesizer that blind screen-reader power users favor for high-speed listening." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/, vote 0-0)
- "There is a divide between novice and expert screen-reader users in voice preference: novices prioritize voice naturalness while experts care little about TTS quality and instead prioritize reliability and application support." (https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3955012, vote 0-0)
- "Expert users routinely speed up TTS output, which makes the voice sound more artificial/robotic, the very quality novices dislike, indicating that at high speed naturalness is sacrificed and is no longer the priority for trained listeners." (https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3955012, vote 0-0)
- "Recognition scores for time-compressed (TC) speech systematically over-estimate recognition of naturally-produced fast-rate speech; listeners do substantially worse on natural fast speech than on time-compressed speech at a comparable rate, so a TC voice does not behave like genuinely fast speech." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/, vote 0-0)
- "The intelligibility ranking from best to worst is normal-rate, then 40% time-compressed, then natural fast speech (statistically significant, p <= 0.006), establishing that uniform time-compression is more intelligible than naturally fast speech at matched rate." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/, vote 0-0)
- "Natural fast speech degrades intelligibility because it introduces segmental distortions (deletions and distortions of stops/affricates from co-articulation and lenition/target undershoot) that uniform time-scale compression does not introduce, since TC preserves spectral information and temporal fine structure at a reduced time scale." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/, vote 0-0)
- "Linearly time-compressed normal-rate speech is processed faster and is preferred by listeners over naturally produced fast speech, even when both are perfectly intelligible — i.e., the natural-speech 'reduction' that occurs when a human speaks fast is a net liability, not an asset. This directly undercuts the assumption that training a TTS to natively produce human-style fast speech would beat simply time-compressing a normal voice." (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092, vote 0-0)
- "The processing advantage of linear time-compression over natural fast speech is quantified: in a phoneme-detection task, mean detection time was 572 ms for linear compression vs. 624 ms for natural fast speech — a statistically significant 52 ms advantage (copy-fast-speech-timing was intermediate at 600 ms)." (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092, vote 0-0)
- "Making artificially time-compressed speech imitate the non-linear timing pattern of natural fast speech (compressing unstressed syllables more than stressed ones — e.g. stressed to 65%, unstressed to 45%) SLOWS word processing rather than improving it; prior work found it actually hurt intelligibility relative to plain linear compression. So investing in prosody-mimicking 'rate-aware' generation that copies human fast-speech timing is likely wasted effort." (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092, vote 0-0)
- "Combining Lombard speaking-style conversion with SSDRC DSP processing (Lombard-SSDRC TTS) yields large objective intelligibility gains over a normal/baseline TTS voice, measured by SIIB-Gauss: 110-130% relative improvement in speech-shaped noise and 47-140% in competing-speaker noise." (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2008.05809, vote 0-0)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: This is a synthesis task. I have all the verified claims and need to merge duplicates, group into findings, and produce structured output. No file investigation needed — the task is pure synthesis of the provided adversarially-verified claims.

input.summary: For a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (Kokoro) optimized for high-speed listening, the strongest evidence points to a counterintuitive bottom line: post-hoc time-compression of a normal-rate voice generally beats both naturally-produced fast speech AND deliberately hyperarticulated "clear" speech at aggressive rates, which means investing in fine-tuning on clear-speech/Lombard corpora to natively generate fast speech is poorly supported and likely not worth the cost. The science of why fast speech stays intelligible is well established: vowel identity is carried by dynamic formant MOVEMENT (not static targets), fricative/consonant clarity depends on spectral energy shifting to higher frequencies, longer vowel duration buys processing time, and comprehension lives in a spectro-temporal "core" of temporal modulations ~1-8 Hz and spectral modulations below ~4 cycles/kHz. The single most actionable, well-evidenced intervention for extreme speed is rhythmic restructuring ("repackaging" — inserting silent gaps to keep the packet rate near the ~5 packets/s theta center), which restores intelligibility of time-compressed speech and works on ANY engine's output, making it a high-value, low-cost DSP tier. For effort tiering: pick/configure a voice and add rate-aware DSP (time-compression + repackaging + high-frequency/consonant spectral shaping) before considering any fine-tune; the evidence does not justify training from scratch or fine-tuning on clear-speech data for the speed-optimized track. Note that several engine-specific recommendations, blind-power-user formant-synthesizer preference claims, and the Lombard+SSDRC DSP gain figures did NOT survive verification (0 votes or refuted), so engine head-to-head guidance and the formant-vs-neural preference remain evidence-thin and should be validated with your own listening tests.

findings.claim: Post-hoc time-compression of a normal-rate voice is more intelligible than naturally-produced fast speech AND more intelligible than deliberately hyperarticulated 'clear' speech, with the clear-speech advantage inverting at aggressive compression rates. This directly undercuts the case for fine-tuning a neural TTS on clear-speech/Lombard corpora to natively generate fast speech.
findings.evidence: Valentini-Botinhao et al. (arXiv:1901.07239, Edinburgh CSTR): 'compressed normal speech is more intelligible than compressed clear speech even when the overall duration of speech and silence is kept the same'; 'unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech.' The synthetic/TTS extension (Speech Communication S0167639315000977) found 'generating speech at a normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates' for English voices. Janse et al. and the 2016 Springer study corroborate that natural fast speech is harder to process than linearly compressed speech, with perceptual adaptation transferring only from compressed to natural, not vice versa. NUANCE: Krause & Braida (2002, JASA) show NATURALLY-produced clear speech keeps its advantage at faster natural rates — a different mechanism (natural production) than signal-domain compression of pre-recorded slow clear speech. The advantage of normal-then-compress also depends on how cleanly any fast version is articulated. ACTIONABLE: favor generating at normal rate then time-compressing; do NOT invest in clear-speech/Lombard fine-tuning for the fast track on the strength of this evidence.

findings.claim: Rhythmic restructuring ('repackaging' — inserting silent gaps into time-compressed speech to keep the packet rate near the brain's theta center, ~5 packets/second for young adults) restores intelligibility at high speed. Rhythm, not raw rate alone, governs comprehensibility, and this is an engine-agnostic DSP intervention.
findings.evidence: JASA (Oct 2018, PMC6181647): the 'rescue point' — earliest rate at which repackaging beats continuous time-compression — was 5 packets/s for young adults (center of the 3-8 Hz theta range) and 4 packets/s for older adults. Repackaged speech at the same information rate is markedly more intelligible than continuously compressed speech (Ghitza/Greenberg line: ~50% WER without gaps, restored with 20-120 ms inserted gaps). The 2023 Nature Sci Reports paper qualifies the MECHANISM (temporal-fine-structure may contribute alongside theta synchronization) but does not contradict the behavioral rescue-point finding. CAVEAT: theta-oscillation CAUSATION is the TEMPO hypothesis (correlational framework), not proven causation — but the load-bearing facts (5 packets/s rescue point; rhythm > raw rate) are directly sourced. ACTIONABLE: this is a cheap, high-value DSP-tier intervention applicable to ANY engine's output (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG), especially for the speed-optimized power-listener track. NOTE: a related claim that theta sets a hard 'neural ceiling' time-compression cannot overcome was REFUTED (1-2).

findings.claim: Vowel identity at speed is carried by dynamic formant MOVEMENT (linear formant transition between vowel-nucleus endpoints), not by static formant targets; preserving formant transitions is what carries vowel intelligibility.
findings.evidence: Neel/Ferguson & Quené (JASA 2014, PMC4048446): linear formant movement between 20%-80% nucleus endpoints (VL) had beta=0.576, p<0.0001 on vowel intelligibility, while non-linear within-nucleus deviation (TLadj) was non-significant. Held for both young normal-hearing and elderly hearing-impaired listeners. Corroborated by the broader Vowel Inherent Spectral Change (VISC) literature: dynamic-change models classify vowels far better than steady-state models, and flat-formant vowels show large identification drops. ACTIONABLE for DSP angle (4): content-aware per-phoneme compression should compress steady-state vowel portions MORE and preserve consonant-vowel transitions and formant movement, since the movement carries identity.

findings.claim: Longer vowel duration improves intelligibility (giving more processing time), and clear speech lengthens vowels by ~1.5x (290 ms vs 204 ms) — relevant to why aggressive uniform compression hurts and why variable-rate compression that preserves perceptual processing time helps.
findings.evidence: Ferguson & Quené (JASA 135(6):3570-3584, 2014, PMC4048446): 'Longer vowel duration helped both groups but especially EHI listeners, perhaps by allowing more processing time'; average vowel durations 290 ms (clear) vs 204 ms (conversational), a 1.5x increase. Foundational, decades-replicated (cf. Picheny et al. 1986). NUANCE: the per-vowel duration effect on identification was significant 'only for tense vowels.' IMPLICATION: aggressive uniform time-compression that shortens vowels below processing-time thresholds is a key intelligibility cost — supports content-aware variable-rate compression over flat compression.

findings.claim: Clear-speech / hyperarticulation expands the vowel space (front-vowel F2 up, back-vowel F2 down; F2 range +~14%, F1 range +~9%), and this expansion aids intelligibility for normal-hearing listeners — quantifying the hyperarticulation effect for the general-student default voice.
findings.evidence: Ferguson & Quené (PMC4048446): F2 range expanded 14% (0.38 Bark, t(40)=5.6, p<0.0001); F1 range expanded 9% (0.23 Bark, t(40)=4.3, p=0.0001); front vowels showed higher F2, back vowels lower F2 in clear speech. NUANCE: expansion improved vowel intelligibility for young normal-hearing listeners but NOT for elderly hearing-impaired listeners. RELEVANCE: supports vowel-space expansion as a property of the natural default voice for general students; but note this is about NATURALLY-produced clear speech and does NOT contradict Finding 0 (vowel-space expansion does not survive signal-domain compression to the same advantage).

findings.claim: Deliberate hyperarticulation (clear speech) produces a measurable intelligibility advantage for consonants/fricatives over conversational speech — historically 7 to 38 percentage points — and lowers SNR identification thresholds for both normal-hearing and simulated-hearing-impaired listeners.
findings.evidence: Maniwa, Jongman & Wade (Interspeech 2007; JASA 2008 123(2):1114-1125): clear speech yields 'intelligibility advantages relative to conversational speech ranging from 7 to 38 percentage points.' SNR-threshold benefit confirmed for 14 normal-hearing + 14 simulated-impaired listeners on fricative minimal pairs. NUANCE: for impaired listeners the advantage was overall but NOT universal across all fricative pairs (non-sibilant pairs unreliable); the same paper flags robustness caveats (negative clear-speech benefits seen in some elderly hearing-impaired listeners elsewhere). CONTEXT: this is the magnitude of the natural clear-speech effect — but per Finding 0 this advantage does not survive aggressive signal-domain time-compression, so it argues for a natural default voice, not for clear-speech-based fast generation.

findings.claim: Fricative/consonant clear-speech intelligibility for normal-hearing listeners is driven mainly by a shift of spectral energy toward HIGHER frequencies — a directly actionable target for DSP consonant-boosting / spectral shaping.
findings.evidence: Maniwa, Jongman & Wade (2007/2008): significant positive correlations (p<0.05) between intelligibility advantage and DFT/LPC spectral-peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1); negative for M3. 'A shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement.' IMPORTANT SCOPE LIMIT: this holds for NORMAL-HEARING listeners; simulated-hearing-impaired listeners benefited from LOWER-frequency cues instead — so a high-frequency boost is not universally safe. ACTIONABLE (DSP angle 4): high-frequency emphasis / consonant boosting on fricatives is an evidence-backed spectral-shaping target for the normal-hearing student/power-listener audience.

findings.claim: Speech comprehension lives in a core spectro-temporal modulation region: temporal modulations ~1-7 Hz (bandpass, peaking near the syllabic rate) and spectral modulations below ~1-4 cycles/kHz (low-pass); comprehension is significantly impaired when modulations below ~8 Hz temporal / ~4 cycles/kHz spectral are removed. This defines what intelligibility metrics and modulation-envelope enhancement must preserve.
findings.evidence: Elliott & Theunissen (2009, PLoS Comput Biol 5(3):e1000302, UC Berkeley): 'the MTF was bandpass in temporal modulations and low-pass in spectral modulations: temporal modulations from 1 to 7 Hz and spectral modulations <1 cycles/kHz were the most important'; and there exists a 'core' of modulations below ~4 cycles/kHz and ~8 Hz essential for comprehension, with comprehension significantly impaired when temporal modulations <12 Hz or spectral modulations <4 cycles/kHz are removed. Consistent with the STI/Houtgast-Steeneken tradition and the ~4 Hz syllabic peak. ACTIONABLE (angles 4 & 6): modulation-envelope enhancement should preserve/strengthen this 1-8 Hz temporal band (which is exactly why time-compression that pushes the syllabic modulation rate too high degrades intelligibility, and why repackaging — restoring ~5 Hz packet rhythm — rescues it); modulation-spectrum analysis and STMI/STOI are the right objective metrics to gate a 'significant gain' decision.

input.caveats:
SCOPE OF EVIDENCE vs. THE SIX ANGLES: The verified claims strongly cover the SCIENCE (angle 1 acoustic/prosodic properties; angle 4 DSP targets) and the CORE TRADE-OFF (angle 3: time-compression beats native-fast generation and beats clear-speech generation under aggressive compression). They do NOT well-cover several requested angles. (1) ANGLE 2 (formant synthesis vs neural; blind power-user preference for Eloquence/DECtalk/eSpeak-NG): ALL supporting claims scored 0-0 (unverified) or were not confirmed — including the 22-syll/s blind-listener ceiling, the use of Eloquence in those experiments, and the novice-vs-expert naturalness-vs-reliability divide. Treat the formant-vs-neural preference and the quantified naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off as UNRESOLVED by this research. (2) ANGLE 5 (head-to-head engine/voice comparison: Kokoro/Piper/eSpeak-NG/RHVoice/Azure/Google/ElevenLabs and specific config knobs): NO claim survived verification; there is no evidence-backed engine ranking here — you must run your own listening tests. (3) ANGLE 6 (measurement): supported only indirectly via the modulation-core finding; STOI/STMI/MUSHRA protocol specifics were not independently verified as claims, though the modulation-spectrum target (1-8 Hz temporal, <4 cycles/kHz spectral) is solid.

MECHANISM vs. SIGNAL-PROCESSING DISTINCTION: Findings 4-6 (vowel-space expansion, fricative spectral shift, 7-38 pt clear-speech gain) describe NATURALLY-PRODUCED clear speech and support a natural default voice — but Finding 0 shows these advantages do NOT survive signal-domain time-compression and can invert. Do not conflate 'clear speech is good' with 'fine-tune on clear-speech corpora for the fast track' — the evidence specifically argues against the latter.

HEARING-PROFILE DEPENDENCE: Several effects (vowel-space expansion benefit, high-frequency fricative boost) hold for NORMAL-HEARING listeners but reverse or vanish for hearing-impaired listeners. Recommendations target normal-hearing students/power-listeners.

CAUSATION HEDGE: The theta-oscillation account behind repackaging is a correlational framework (TEMPO hypothesis), not proven causation; the behavioral rescue-point result is solid regardless. The claimed hard 'neural ceiling' from theta was REFUTED.

RECENCY: Core psychoacoustics/phonetics sources (2007-2018) are foundational and stable, not staleness-sensitive. However, the TTS-ENGINE landscape (Kokoro, Piper, neural-voice quality, on-device inference) IS fast-moving and the synthesis contains NO verified current engine guidance — any engine/config recommendation must be re-validated empirically.

EFFORT-TIER BOTTOM LINE (synthesized, not separately voted): Tier 1 (pick/configure voice) + Tier 2 (DSP: time-compress normal-rate output, repackage to ~5 packets/s, high-frequency/consonant spectral shaping, content-aware vowel-vs-transition variable-rate compression) carry the strongest evidence-to-cost ratio. Tier 3 (fine-tune on clear-speech/Lombard) is NOT supported and likely not worth it for the fast track. Tier 4 (train from scratch) is unjustified by any verified evidence. Gate any fine-tune decision behind a measured 'significant gain' bar using modulation-spectrum/STOI-STMI metrics plus A/B or MUSHRA listening tests.
openQuestions: Do blind/screen-reader power users actually prefer formant synthesizers (Eloquence/DECtalk/eSpeak-NG) over neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second, and by how much? All supporting claims were unverified (0-0) — this angle needs primary listening-study evidence before acting on it.

Which specific local/open-source engine and voice (Kokoro vs Piper vs eSpeak-NG vs RHVoice) is most intelligible at high speed, and what exact config knobs (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) help? No engine head-to-head survived verification — requires an empirical bake-off on your own pipeline.

How much MEASURABLE comprehensibility does repackaging + spectral shaping + content-aware variable-rate compression add over plain linear time-compression of Kokoro output, measured with STOI/STMI and a MUSHRA/A-B panel — i.e., does the DSP tier alone clear a 'significant gain' bar so that fine-tuning is provably unnecessary?

Does any neural-TTS native rate-control or speaking-rate conditioning (as opposed to clear-speech fine-tuning) ever beat normal-rate-then-compress for synthetic voices specifically? The confirmed evidence concerns natural human speech and one synthetic study; a Kokoro-specific test of native rate conditioning vs post-hoc compression is unaddressed.

content.text:
I've completed the synthesis. The structured output captures the 10 verified claims merged into 8 coherent findings, all high-confidence given the unanimous votes and primary peer-reviewed sources.

Key takeaways from the synthesis:

- **The central decision (fine-tune vs. compress):** The strongest evidence argues AGAINST investing in clear-speech/Lombard fine-tuning for the fast track. Time-compressing a normal-rate voice beats both naturally-produced fast speech and clear speech at aggressive rates.
- **The highest-value cheap intervention:** Repackaging (inserting silent gaps to hold ~5 packets/s) restores intelligibility at speed and works on any engine's output.
- **The science targets** for DSP: preserve dynamic formant movement, compress steady-state vowels more than transitions, shift fricative energy to higher frequencies, and protect the 1-8 Hz temporal / <4 cycles/kHz spectral modulation core.

I flagged important gaps honestly: angles 2 (formant-vs-neural / blind power-user preference) and 5 (engine head-to-head) had NO surviving claims, so those need your own empirical validation rather than relying on this research.
